claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances. Let me start with a few key questions.

**First, could you tell me: how many children are you inquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my child. I have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 17, the rules are a bit different. To determine if you can claim Child Benefit for them, I need to know:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any approved education or training. They finished school and aren't doing A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex is 17 and not in approved education or training, I need to ask:

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they haven't registered with any careers service or the armed forces.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the rules, since Alex is 17 years old and is **not** in approved education or training, and has **not** registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you would **not** be eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex.

According to the UK government's Child Benefit rules, you can normally claim Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over only if they remain in approved education or training. Alternatively, if they leave education or training, you can continue to claim for up to 20 weeks if they register with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

Since neither of these conditions applies to Alex's situation, Child Benefit eligibility would not continue beyond their 16th birthday.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else I can help you with regarding benefits or any other children you may have?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and not in approved education or training. According to the rules, Child Benefit normally stops when a child reaches 16 unless they stay in approved education or training. The only exception is if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, which allows for 20 weeks of continued payments. Alex has not registered with either of these services, so they do not qualify for this exception. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit. Their child Alex is 17 years old, has left education, and has not registered with a careers service or armed forces. This means Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for children aged 16 or over, which requires either continued approved education/training or registration with a designated service following departure from education."}
